White-bellied Pangolin Sand Cat
🐪
Africa

Dromedary Camel

Camelus dromedarius
Status Domesticated
Habitat Deserts, arid regions
Diet Herbivore - desert vegetation
Lifespan 40-50 years
Weight 400-600 kg
×

The dromedary camel (Camelus dromedarius), also known as the Arabian camel, is the ultimate desert survivor and has been humanity's most valuable companion in arid regions for over 4,000 years. These magnificent one-humped ungulates are perfectly adapted to life in the harsh deserts of North Africa, the Middle East, and the Horn of Africa. Contrary to popular belief, camels do not store water in their humps - the hump is actually a fat reserve that can sustain them for weeks without food. Dromedaries can drink up to 100 liters of water in just 10 minutes and can survive losing up to 30% of their body weight in water without ill effects. Their remarkable adaptations include nostrils that can close against blowing sand, long eyelashes and bushy eyebrows for eye protection, and wide, padded feet that spread on soft sand. These 'ships of the desert' have enabled trade and travel across some of the world's most inhospitable terrain.

Key Facts

  • Can drink 100 liters of water in just 10 minutes
  • Hump stores fat (not water) - can sustain for weeks without food
  • Can survive losing 30% of body weight in water
  • Nostrils close completely to keep out blowing sand
  • Body temperature fluctuates 3-6°C to conserve water
  • Domesticated for over 4,000 years - vital to desert civilizations

Conservation Status

Listed as Not Evaluated by the IUCN as they are domesticated animals. Wild dromedaries are extinct; all current populations are domesticated or feral. Estimates suggest over 15 million dromedaries exist across Africa and the Middle East. They remain economically vital to millions of people for transport, milk, meat, and wool. Feral populations in Australia, descended from imported animals, have become invasive pests. Conservation concerns focus on maintaining genetic diversity in domestic populations and managing feral populations humanely.
